file_name: BOLIVARMININGCORP_05_23_2003-EX-2.1-VISP WEB SITE BUILDING AND HOSTING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall renew automatically each year thereafter, unless either party serves written notice of its intention not to renew, on the other at least 90 days prior to the expiration of the then current term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall renew automatically each year thereafter, unless either party serves written notice of its intention not to renew, on the other at least 10 days prior to the expiration of the then current term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period to 10 days is unreasonable and potentially violates implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, particularly considering the significant investment described in the agreement. Ten days is unlikely to be sufficient time to find a replacement service or provider.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen Oblig Law § 5-903 mandates that automatic renewal clauses in service contracts are only enforceable if the recipient of services is given notice of the impending renewal at least fifteen days, but not more than thirty days, before the date that the service provider is required to give notice of cancellation. Here, a 10 day notice period is shorter than the legally required minimum, thus rendering the automatic renewal clause unenforceable. While the statute specifies a notice between 15 and 30 days, the modified 10-day period fails to meet this requirement.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-903 Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to rec... General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 9 § 5-903. Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to recipient of services. 1. As used in this section, "person" means an individual, firm, company, partnership or corporation. 2. No provision of a contract for service, maintenance or repair to or for any real or personal property which states that the term of the contract shall be deemed renewed for a specified additional period unless the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair gives notice to the person furnishing such contract service, maintenance or repair of his intention to terminate the contract at the expiration of such term, shall be enforceable against the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair, unless the person furnishing the service, maintenance or repair, at least fifteen days and not more than thirty days previous to the time specified for serving such notice upon him, shall give to the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair written notice, served personally or by certified mail, calling the attention of that person to the existence of such provision in the contract. 3. Nothing herein contained shall be construed to apply to a contract in which the automatic renewal period specified is one month or less.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts NY Gen Oblig Law § 5-903, as explained in the Law Explanation, because it reduces the non-renewal notice period to 10 days, which is less than the legally required minimum of 15 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in SECTION 5-903 that '...the person furnishing the service, maintenance or repair, at least fifteen days and not more than thirty days previous to the time specified for serving such notice upon him, shall give to the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair written notice...'. The changed text makes the automatic renewal clause unenforceable by failing to meet this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Once the common stock has been registered, or, after the one year period applicable under Rule 144, whichever occurs first, the Company at its sole cost and expense have its attorney issue an opinion letter for removal of the legend and release all stock transfer instructions on the common stock, except as maybe required under Rule 144.
perturbation.changed_text: Once the common stock has been registered, or, after the one year period applicable under Rule 144, whichever occurs first, the Company may, but is not required to, at its sole cost and expense have its attorney issue an opinion letter for removal of the legend and release all stock transfer instructions on the common stock, except as maybe required under Rule 144.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may' and inserting 'but is not required to', the obligation of the Company to issue an opinion letter is removed. This contradicts the intended benefit of the agreement for YourNetPlus.com (Provider) to receive freely transferable stock after a specified period.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 144 provides a safe harbor for selling restricted securities. The original text of the agreement appears to intend to facilitate the removal of restrictions on the stock received by YourNetPlus.com (Provider) so that they can freely trade it after the holding period. By making the opinion letter optional, the amended clause undercuts the intended benefit of free tradability after a specified time, effectively negating the purpose of including the Rule 144 provision and thus conflicting the spirit of securities regulations designed to ensure fair trading practices after required holding periods are satisfied.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.144 - Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.144 - Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.144 Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. Preliminary Note: Certain basic principles are essential to an understanding of the registration requirements in the Securities Act of 1933 (the Act or the Securities Act) and the purposes underlying Rule 144: 1. If any person sells a non-exempt security to any other person, the sale must be registered unless an exemption can be found for the transaction. 2. Section 4(1) of the Securities Act provides one such exemption for a transaction “by a person other than an issuer , underwriter , or dealer.” Therefore, an understanding of the term “underwriter” is important in determining whether or not the Section 4(1) exemption from registration is available for the sale of the securities. The term “underwriter” is broadly defined in Section 2(a)(11) of the Securities Act to mean any person who has purchased from an issuer with a view to, or offers or sells for an issuer in connection with, the distribution of any security , or participates , or has a direct or indirect participation in any such undertaking, or participates or has a participation in the direct or indirect underwriting of any such undertaking. The interpretation of this definition traditionally has focused on the words “with a view to” in the phrase “purchased from an issuer with a view to * * * distribution .” An investment banking firm which arranges with an issuer for the public sale of its securities is clearly an “underwriter” under that section. However, individual investors who are not professionals in the securities business also may be “underwrit
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' removes a mandatory obligation, creating an inconsistency. The original text requires the company to issue an opinion letter, facilitating the removal of restrictions on the stock. The changed text makes this action optional. The Law Explanation states that this 'undercuts the intended benefit of free tradability after a specified time, effectively negating the purpose of including the Rule 144 provision'. Although the snippets do not directly state this violates Rule 144, they describe its purpose and context, implying that making the removal of restrictions optional undermines this purpose. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the original intent to facilitate free tradability under Rule 144.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of, relating to or in connection with this Agreement that has not been resolved through the informal mediation of the parties shall be resolved through arbitration pursuant to the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association then in effect, as modified by the terms set forth below: (a) the arbitration shall be conducted in New York state; (b) the arbitration shall be conducted by a single arbitrator selected by the parties; (c) the parties shall act in a commercially reasonable manner and speedily select and then conduct the arbitration within 45 days with the expenditure of minimal discovery efforts and expense which shall be determined, if necessary, by the arbitrator.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of, relating to or in connection with this Agreement that has not been resolved through the informal mediation of the parties shall be resolved through arbitration pursuant to the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association then in effect, as modified by the terms set forth below: (a) the arbitration shall be conducted in New York state; (b) the arbitration shall be conducted by a single arbitrator selected solely by Provider; (c) the parties shall act in a commercially reasonable manner and speedily select and then conduct the arbitration within 45 days with the expenditure of minimal discovery efforts and expense which shall be determined, if necessary, by the arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the selection of the arbitrator to be solely by the Provider creates a biased arbitration process that could violate principles of fairness and impartiality, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 10 outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including instances where the arbitrator exhibited evident partiality. While not explicitly stating arbitrator selection, courts have interpreted the FAA to require a fundamentally fair process. Allowing one party to unilaterally select the arbitrator raises serious concerns about bias and impartiality, potentially leading a court to vacate any resulting arbitration award due to 'evident partiality' violating the spirit and intent of the FAA to provide fair and impartial dispute resolution. This can be argued as exceeding their powers and misbehavior.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle of impartiality in arbitration, potentially violating 9 U.S.C. § 10. By allowing the Provider to solely select the arbitrator, the modified contract introduces bias. Scraped Law Snippet 2, mirroring Snippet 1, states that an arbitration award can be vacated if there was 'evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators'. The original text ensured a selection process agreed upon by both parties, promoting fairness, whereas the changed text undermines this fairness, leading to a strong potential for partiality. This unilateral selection power contradicts the intent of the FAA to provide fair dispute resolution, making the arbitration agreement potentially unenforceable.
